---
title: "An LLM-Augmented R/Shiny Platform for Quantitative Systems Pharmacology"
subtitle: "2026 Annual Conference of the Korean Society for Mathematical Biology (KSMB) · Special Session B1 — Mathematical Modeling in New Drug Development & Clinical Pharmacotherapy"
author: "Jueun Kang, Kwang-Won Park, Junghyun Bae, Sangha Han, Jeongmin Park, **Sungpil Han, MD, PhD** (Speaker)"
institute: "¹ Dept. of Pharmacology, College of Medicine, The Catholic University of Korea · ² PIPET, The Catholic University of Korea"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, theme/custom.scss]
    css: theme/fonts.css
    html-math-method: mathml
    slide-number: c/t
    show-slide-number: all
    footer: "An LLM-Augmented QSP Platform · KSMB 2026"
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    margin: 0.055
    center-title-slide: true
    incremental: false
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    highlight-style: github
    embed-resources: true
    fig-align: center
    link-external-icon: false
---

## Roadmap

[KSMB 2026 · Special Session B1]{.kicker}

> **Thesis.** Quantitative Systems Pharmacology (QSP) turns drug action into *large systems of nonlinear ODEs*. Building them is slow and rare. We let an autonomous LLM agent build them — **{{< var n_models >}} disease models and counting.**

1. **The problem** — why drug development fails, and why statistics aren't enough
2. **QSP and its mathematics** — $dx/dt = f(x,\,\theta,\,u)$
3. **The engine** — the Claude Code Routine, an autonomous model-builder
4. **Why an LLM** — tireless, broad, consistent
5. **Scale & breadth** — a living library across ~{{< var n_categories >}} therapeutic areas
6. **Deep dives** — IgA nephropathy · sickle cell · multiple myeloma
7. **Open mathematical problems** — where *this room* comes in

## Drug development is staggeringly expensive — and mostly fails

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
- **~\$1–2.6 billion** and **10–15 years** per approved drug
- **~90%** of candidates entering clinical trials never reach patients
- Failures cluster at two points:
  - **Early** — the target was never mechanistically validated
  - **Late** — unexpected efficacy / safety surprises in pivotal trials

[We need models that **explain and extrapolate**, not just fit.]{.lead}
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
[~90%]{.bignum}<br>
[clinical-phase attrition]{.muted}

<br>

[~\$2.6B]{.bignum}<br>
[cost per approved drug]{.muted}
:::
::::

[DiMasi et al., *J Health Econ* 2016; Wong et al., *Biostatistics* 2019.]{.fineprint}

## The mechanistic gap

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Statistical / empirical models**

- interpolate *within* observed data
- "what" happened — fit to endpoints
- struggle to extrapolate to new doses, populations, combinations
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Mechanistic (QSP) models**

- encode *causal* biology: target → pathway → disease
- "why and how" — simulate the unobserved
- extrapolate across dose · species · subpopulation · regimen
:::
::::

> To predict a drug that does not yet exist, you must model the **mechanism** it will act on.

## What is Quantitative Systems Pharmacology?

QSP = **systems biology × pharmacokinetics/pharmacodynamics** — a mechanistic, mathematical description of the causal chain

$$\textbf{drug} \;\rightarrow\; \textbf{target} \;\rightarrow\; \textbf{pathway} \;\rightarrow\; \textbf{disease} \;\rightarrow\; \textbf{patient}$$

It answers questions statistics cannot:

- If we modulate *this* target, how far downstream does the effect propagate?
- Which **patient subtypes** respond — and why?
- What dose, schedule, or **combination** optimizes the efficacy–safety balance?

## The anatomy of a QSP model

A QSP model is an initial-value problem — a coupled nonlinear dynamical system:

$$\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \mathbf{f}\!\big(\mathbf{x}(t),\, \boldsymbol{\theta},\, \mathbf{u}(t)\big), \qquad \mathbf{x}(0)=\mathbf{x}_0$$

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
**States $\mathbf{x}$**

drug concentrations · receptor occupancy · signaling species · cell populations · biomarkers · endpoints
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$**

rate constants · $EC_{50}$, $E_{max}$, Hill $n$ · clearances · binding affinities
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Inputs $\mathbf{u}(t)$**

dosing regimens (amount, route, schedule) — the controllable handles of therapy
:::
::::

[Our library: **15–35+ coupled ODEs** and **70–100+ parameters** per disease.]{.lead}

## The recurring nonlinear structures

The same mathematical motifs appear across every disease — a shared grammar:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
**Hill / $E_{max}$ pharmacodynamics**
$$E(C)=\frac{E_{max}\,C^{\,n}}{EC_{50}^{\,n}+C^{\,n}}$$

**Indirect-response (turnover)**
$$\frac{dR}{dt}=k_{in}\,f(\cdot)-k_{out}\,R$$

**Logistic / carrying-capacity growth**
$$\frac{dN}{dt}=rN\!\left(1-\frac{N}{K}\right)-\mathrm{kill}(C)\,N$$
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Mass-action coupling** (immune-complex formation)
$$\frac{d[IC]}{dt}=k_f\,[A][B]-k_c\,[IC]$$

**Target-mediated drug disposition (TMDD)**
$$\frac{d[D]}{dt}=-k_{on}[D][T]+k_{off}[DT]-\cdots$$

**Power-law feedback** & **threshold switching**
$$\mathrm{EPO}\propto\!\Big(\tfrac{Hb_0}{Hb}\Big)^{\!\gamma};\quad \dot E_{es}=\pm\,g(\cdot)$$
:::
::::

[Plus Bliss-independence combinations, transit compartments, and stiff multi-timescale coupling.]{.fineprint}

## Why QSP matters — Model-Informed Drug Development

| Stage | What QSP contributes | Effect on attrition |
|---|---|---|
| Target validation | does target modulation reach the phenotype? bypass / resistance | kills implausible targets early |
| Mechanism of action | quantifies the causal structure; biomarker strategy | stronger evidence packages |
| First-in-human dose | exposure–response & safety margins from mechanism | safer, leaner Phase 1 |
| Trial design | virtual patients → enrichment, endpoints, sample size | higher trial success |
| Translation | animal→human, adult→pediatric extrapolation | better external validity |
| Combinations | synergy / antagonism *in silico* | rational polypharmacy |

[Regulators (FDA / EMA) actively endorse MIDD. QSP lets programs **fail fast, succeed strategically.**]{.lead}

## The bottleneck

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
A single QSP model traditionally costs **expert-months to years**:

- deep literature synthesis across pathophysiology + pharmacology
- careful ODE formulation and parameterization
- software implementation, calibration, visualization

**Consequences**

- only a handful of diseases ever get one
- models are siloed, rarely open, rarely reproducible
- the long tail of diseases is left unmodeled
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
> **One disease ≈ one (or more) expert-years.**
>
> Scarce · slow · closed.
:::
::::

## Thesis — LLM-augmented model generation

> Use an **autonomous LLM coding agent** to build complete, quality-gated, fully-referenced QSP models — *at the pace of one disease per day* — with every artifact under version control.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Each model ships four artifacts**

- 🗺️ Graphviz **mechanistic map**
- ⚙️ `mrgsolve` **ODE model**
- 📊 **Shiny** dashboard
- 📚 curated **references**
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Built-in principles**

- enforced **quality gates**
- **literature grounding** for every parameter
- standardized schema → comparable & auditable
- **reproducible**: code + git history
:::
::::

## Architecture

![From an autonomous agent to four interoperable artifacts, committed to git.](assets/plots/pipeline.png){width="92%"}

## The Claude Code Routine — an autonomous daily loop ★

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](assets/plots/ccr_loop.png){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
A scheduled coding agent. **Each run completes one disease end-to-end and pushes it.**

1. **Select** an uncovered disease (rotate categories)
2. **Research** — synthesize ~50 PubMed sources
3. **Map** — Graphviz network (≥100 nodes)
4. **Model** — `mrgsolve` ODEs (≥15 compartments)
5. **Dashboard** — Shiny (≥6 tabs)
6. **Ground** — sectioned references (≥30)
7. **Commit & push** — automatically

[A git **stop-hook** refuses to end a session until everything is committed & pushed — *the work is never left half-done.*]{.lead}
:::
::::

## Inside one session — the guardrails ★

How do we trust code an LLM wrote? **Structure + grounding + verification.**

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Quality gates (hard minimums)**

- mechanistic map: **≥100 nodes**, **≥8 clusters**
- ODE model: **≥15 compartments**, **≥5 scenarios**
- dashboard: **≥6 tabs**
- references: **≥30 PubMed-indexed**

**Structural templates**

- reusable ODE motifs (Hill, turnover, TMDD) shrink the space of "things that can go wrong"
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Literature grounding**

- every parameter set carries a calibration memo citing trials → the principal **anti-hallucination** mechanism

**Reproducibility & review**

- everything is code in **git**: diffable, auditable, re-runnable
- **human-in-the-loop** scientific review before use
:::
::::

## Why an LLM/AI is the right engine ★

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Tireless & continuous** — runs 24/7, no fatigue; a complete, multi-artifact disease model **nearly every day** — a cadence no human team sustains
- **Vast literature synthesis** — integrates **~{{< var n_references >}} citations** (~{{< var refs_per_model >}}/model), reading & organizing evidence far faster than manual review
- **Breadth across diseases & drugs** — {{< var n_models >}} diseases and **hundreds of drugs/targets** in one framework
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Consistency & standardization** — identical schema and quality bar for every model → comparable, reusable
- **Speed & economics** — expert-months → **hours**
- **Democratization** — brings scarce systems-pharmacology expertise to the **long tail** of diseases that would never get a hand-built model

[The scarce resource is modeling expertise. The LLM *scales* it.]{.lead}
:::
::::

## Deliverable ① — the mechanistic map (graph theory)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="46%"}
![A QSP map is a clustered directed multigraph.](assets/figs/ra_map.png){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="54%"}
- **Vertices** = species / states; **edges** = mechanistic interactions (activation, inhibition, mass transfer)
- **Clusters** = pathway modules — a community structure over the graph
- Built with **Graphviz**; rendered to SVG / PNG and version-controlled

[~{{< var clusters_per_model >}}]{.bignum} **pathway modules per model** — up to {{< var max_nodes >}} graph elements each.
:::
::::

## Deliverable ② — the `mrgsolve` ODE system

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
Real excerpt — IgA nephropathy "four-hit" cascade:

```r
// Hit 3: immune-complex formation (mass action)
dxdt_IC_mes = k_form*GdIgA1*AutoAb - k_clear*IC_mes;
// complement amplification (iptacopan blocks)
dxdt_CompAP = k_syn*IC_mes*(1-E_ipt) - k_deg*CompAP;
// Hill drug effect; Bliss-independent combination
E_bud = Emax*pow(C,h)/(pow(EC50,h)+pow(C,h));
E_muc = 1-(1-E_bud)*(1-E_sib);
```
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
- compiled **C++** solver (stiff systems, event-based dosing)
- couples **drug PK** → **disease PD** → **clinical endpoints**
- **15–35+** state variables per model
- **≥5** therapeutic scenarios each

[Genuine, runnable systems pharmacology — not pseudocode.]{.lead}
:::
::::

## Deliverables ③ + ④ — interaction & evidence

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
**③ Shiny dashboard** — interrogate the model without code

- patient profile · PK · pathophysiology
- clinical endpoints · scenario comparison · biomarkers (6–8 tabs)

![](assets/shiny/merigolix_dashboard.webp){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**④ Reference grounding** — traceable evidence

- **~{{< var refs_per_model >}}** PubMed citations per model, sectioned by mechanism
- every parameter & claim anchored to the literature
- **~{{< var n_references >}}** references across the library

[Grounding is what separates a model from a guess.]{.lead}
:::
::::

## From biology to runnable code — `mrgsolve`

Each model is a compact, C++-compiled ODE program (Merigolix-flavoured excerpt):

```cpp
[PARAM] CL=549, Vc=1690, Ka=3.4, IC50=1.5, kin=3.8, kout=0.066
[CMT]   GUT, CENT, E2, LESION
[ODE]
double Cp   = CENT/Vc;                    // plasma drug
double inh  = Imax*Cp/(IC50 + Cp);        // Hill inhibition
dxdt_GUT    = -Ka*GUT;
dxdt_CENT   =  Ka*GUT - (CL/Vc)*CENT;
dxdt_E2     =  kin*(1 - inh) - kout*E2;   // estradiol turnover
dxdt_LESION =  kg*E2*LESION - kreg*LESION;// E2-driven lesion growth
```

[~40 lines define a full PK → hormone → disease model.]{.lead}

## From model to interactive app — `Shiny`

A reactive server turns parameter sliders into live clinical predictions:

```r
server <- function(input, output) {
  sim <- reactive({
    mod |> param(IC50 = input$ic50, CL = input$cl) |>
      ev(amt = input$dose, ii = 24, addl = input$days) |>
      mrgsim(end = 168)
  })
  output$e2   <- renderPlot(plot(sim(), E2   ~ time))
  output$pain <- renderPlot(plot(sim(), PAIN ~ time))
}
```

[Move a slider → re-solve the ODEs → E2, pain, BMD update instantly.]{.lead}

## The Claude Code Routine — in pseudocode

```python
while True:                       # runs on a schedule, ~daily
    disease = pick_uncovered(rotate_categories=True)
    refs    = read_pubmed(disease, n=50)             # broad literature sweep
    cmap    = build_graphviz_map(disease, refs)      # >= 100 nodes
    model   = write_mrgsolve_odes(disease, refs)     # >= 15 ODEs
    app     = build_shiny_dashboard(model)           # >= 6 tabs
    assert quality_gates(cmap, model, app, refs)     # hard minimums
    git_commit_and_push(disease)                     # stop-hook enforced
```

[One disease, end-to-end, every run — and it never tires.]{.lead}

## A living library, growing daily

```{=html}
<div class="stat-grid">
  <div class="stat-card"><div class="n">191</div><div class="l">disease models</div></div>
  <div class="stat-card"><div class="n">~15</div><div class="l">therapeutic areas</div></div>
  <div class="stat-card"><div class="n">192</div><div class="l">ODE systems</div></div>
  <div class="stat-card"><div class="n">~2,200</div><div class="l">pathway clusters</div></div>
  <div class="stat-card"><div class="n">~9,700</div><div class="l">PubMed references</div></div>
  <div class="stat-card"><div class="n">+1 / day</div><div class="l">and still growing</div></div>
</div>
```

[Each is a complete, runnable, fully-referenced QSP model.]{.lead}

## The gallery wall

![A fraction of the {{< var n_models >}} mechanistic maps — one autonomous build each.](assets/figs/montage.png){width="86%"}

## Breadth of coverage

One consistent framework, spanning the spectrum of human disease:

[Oncology — solid]{.chip} · [Oncology — heme]{.chip} · [Autoimmune / rheumatic]{.chip} · [Vasculitis]{.chip} · [Cardiovascular]{.chip} · [Respiratory]{.chip} · [Renal / urologic]{.chip} · [GI / hepatobiliary]{.chip} · [Endocrine / metabolic]{.chip} · [Neurologic]{.chip} · [Psychiatric]{.chip} · [Dermatologic]{.chip} · [Infectious]{.chip} · [Ophthalmic]{.chip} · [Rare / genetic]{.chip}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Cancers** — breast, NSCLC, SCLC, glioblastoma, CML, multiple myeloma, melanoma, pancreatic…
- **Rare & genetic** — Fabry, Gaucher, DMD, SMA, Huntington, transthyretin amyloidosis…
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Common chronic** — diabetes, heart failure, COPD, CKD…
- **Immune / hematologic** — lupus, RA, sickle cell, ITP, myelofibrosis…

Hundreds of distinct **drugs and molecular targets**.
:::
::::

## Case vignettes — one modeling insight each (1 / 2)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
**NSCLC — EGFR resistance**

Osimertinib vs the **T790M / C797S** escape clones: the model times resistant-clone outgrowth → rationale for combination or sequencing.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**HFrEF — GDMT sequencing**

ARNI · β-blocker · MRA · SGLT2i: explore **order and up-titration speed** against reverse remodeling (LVEF, NT-proBNP).
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**ATTR amyloidosis — gene silencing**

TTR knockdown (patisiran / vutrisiran) → tetramer–monomer kinetics → neuropathy and cardiac burden over years.
:::
::::

## Case vignettes — one modeling insight each (2 / 2)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
**SLE — type-I interferon**

Anifrolumab (anti-IFNAR) damps the IFN signature; the model links IFN → autoantibody → flare risk (SLEDAI).
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**T2DM — incretin axis**

GLP-1 / GIP (tirzepatide): a glucose–insulin–weight feedback loop → HbA1c and body-weight trajectories.
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**ITP — platelet kinetics**

TPO-receptor agonists vs anti-platelet antibody: a production-versus-destruction balance → platelet recovery.
:::
::::

## Drugs · targets · modeled endpoints

A single framework spans dozens of drug classes and mechanisms of action:

| Disease | Drug (example) | Target / mechanism | Modeled endpoint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rheumatoid arthritis | tocilizumab | IL-6 receptor | DAS28 · CRP |
| Type 2 diabetes | semaglutide | GLP-1 receptor | HbA1c · weight |
| Heart failure (rEF) | sacubitril/valsartan | neprilysin + AT₁ (ARNI) | LVEF · NT-proBNP |
| Non-small-cell lung cancer | osimertinib | mutant EGFR | tumor burden |
| Pulmonary arterial HTN | macitentan | endothelin A/B receptor | PVR · 6-min walk |
| IgA nephropathy | sparsentan | endothelin-A + AT₁ | UPCR · eGFR |
| Sickle cell disease | voxelotor | HbS–O₂ affinity | Hb · hemolysis |
| Multiple myeloma | daratumumab | CD38 (TMDD) | M-protein |

[Each drug–target–endpoint link is grounded in the trial literature.]{.lead}

## IgA nephropathy — the "four-hit" cascade

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
![](assets/figs/igan_map.png){width="100%"}

[The most common primary glomerulonephritis worldwide; up to ~30% reach kidney failure within 20 years.]{.fineprint}
:::
::: {.column width="56%"}
**Pathophysiology.** Galactose-deficient IgA1 (Hit 1) → anti-glycan autoantibodies (Hit 2) → circulating immune complexes (Hit 3) → mesangial deposition + alternative-complement activation (Hit 4) → mesangial/podocyte injury, proteinuria, falling eGFR.

**Modeled therapeutics → targets**

- RAAS blockade (ACEi/ARB) — intraglomerular pressure
- Budesonide-TRF — gut-mucosal B cells / APRIL
- Sparsentan — dual endothelin-A + AT₁ receptor
- Iptacopan — complement factor B (alternative pathway)
- Sibeprenlimab — anti-APRIL (upstream Gd-IgA1)

**Endpoints:** UPCR, eGFR slope — calibrated to **NefIgArd, PROTECT, APPLAUSE-IgAN**. *(20 ODEs · 7 scenarios)*
:::
::::

## Sickle cell disease — polymerization to vaso-occlusion

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
![](assets/figs/scd_map.png){width="100%"}

[A single β-globin point mutation (Glu6Val) with lifelong multi-organ consequences.]{.fineprint}
:::
::: {.column width="56%"}
**Pathophysiology.** Deoxygenated HbS polymerizes → red-cell sickling & hemolysis → cell-free hemoglobin scavenges nitric oxide → endothelial activation and P-selectin adhesion → **vaso-occlusive crises**, chronic anemia, progressive organ damage.

**Modeled therapeutics → targets**

- Hydroxyurea — fetal-hemoglobin induction (via BCL11A)
- Voxelotor — raises HbS–O₂ affinity (anti-polymerization)
- Crizanlizumab — anti-P-selectin (anti-adhesion)
- L-glutamine — red-cell oxidative stress

**Endpoints:** total Hb, HbF %, vaso-occlusion rate, LDH — calibrated to **MSH, HOPE, SUSTAIN**. *(24 ODEs; power-law erythropoiesis feedback)*
:::
::::

## Multiple myeloma — an oncology exemplar

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="44%"}
![](assets/figs/mm_map.png){width="100%"}

[Clonal plasma-cell malignancy — the classic **CRAB** features: hyperCalcemia, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone lesions.]{.fineprint}
:::
::: {.column width="56%"}
**Pathophysiology.** Marrow plasma-cell clones secrete monoclonal protein; IL-6 drives autocrine growth; a RANKL / OPG / DKK1 imbalance produces osteolytic bone disease and immunoparesis.

**Modeled therapeutics → targets**

- Bortezomib / carfilzomib — proteasome
- Lenalidomide — immunomodulatory (cereblon)
- Daratumumab — anti-CD38 (**TMDD** kinetics)
- Venetoclax — BCL-2 (t(11;14)); zoledronate — bone

$$\dot N = rN\!\Big(1-\tfrac{N}{K}\Big)(1{+}k_{IL6}\,IL6)-\mathrm{kill}(C)\,N-k_{res}N$$

**Endpoints:** M-protein, free light chains — regimens **VRd / DRd / KRd / DVRd**.
:::
::::

## Simulation snapshots — illustrative model dynamics

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
**IgA nephropathy**
![](assets/plots/plot_igan.png){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Sickle cell disease**
![](assets/plots/plot_scd.png){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
**Multiple myeloma**
![](assets/plots/plot_mm.png){width="100%"}
:::
::::

[Reduced-model trajectories (treated vs. untreated). The full `mrgsolve` models drive the interactive Shiny dashboards.]{.fineprint}

## Flagship case — Merigolix, a GnRH antagonist for endometriosis

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
![](assets/shiny/merigolix_dashboard.webp){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
**Mechanism.** An oral GnRH-receptor antagonist lowers LH/FSH → suppresses ovarian **estradiol (E2)** → regression of estrogen-dependent endometriotic lesions and pain.

**One model couples**

- 2-compartment **PK** (Ka, CL/F, Vc, Q, Vp)
- HPG-axis **PD** — E2 / LH / FSH turnover with Hill inhibition
- **endpoints** — pain (NRS), lesion size, hot flashes, **BMD**, endometrium

[Drug → hormone → lesion → symptom, in one ODE system.]{.lead}
:::
::::

## Merigolix — the estrogen "threshold" trade-off

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**The therapeutic window** (the estrogen-threshold hypothesis)

- too little E2 suppression → no symptom relief
- too much → **hot flashes** and **bone loss**
- aim for a *partial* E2 band (~20–40 pg/mL)

The model makes it quantitative: dose → E2 trough → simultaneous **benefit** (pain ↓, lesion ↓) and **risk** (BMD ↓, flashes ↑).
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**This simulation run**

- E2 trough **2.6 pg/mL**; pain **3.1 → 1.1**; lesion **18.5 → 5.3 mm**
- but **BMD −1.22%** and hot flashes **5.4 → 7.9 / day**
- → motivates **dose titration** and **hormonal add-back**

[QSP turns a clinical trade-off into a tunable optimization.]{.lead}
:::
::::

## Explore the live app

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="68%"}
![](assets/shiny/merigolix_dashboard.webp){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="32%"}
**The dashboard is live and public.**

Set dose, frequency, cycle timing, IC50 / CL multipliers → the ODEs re-solve and every endpoint updates.

![](assets/figs/app_qr.png){width="55%"}

[**[pipetqsp.shinyapps.io/merigolix](https://pipetqsp.shinyapps.io/merigolix/)**]{.lead}

[Scan or click to open it in your browser.]{.fineprint}
:::
::::

## The mathematics of the *whole* library

![Distributions across {{< var n_models >}} models: pathway clusters, references, and graph size.](assets/plots/meta_dist.png){width="92%"}

[A standardized corpus of nonlinear dynamical systems — itself an object worth studying.]{.fineprint}

## Open mathematical problems — a collaboration invitation

A corpus of ~{{< var n_models >}} nonlinear ODE systems is a playground for applied mathematics:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Identifiability** — structural & practical: which $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ are recoverable from feasible measurements?
- **Sensitivity analysis** — global (Sobol, Morris) ranking of drivers
- **Model reduction** — timescale separation, QSSA, balanced truncation of {15–35}-D systems
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
- **Uncertainty quantification** — parameter & structural uncertainty → prediction intervals
- **Dynamics** — stability, bifurcations, thresholds (adaptive → maladaptive RV failure in PAH)
- **Inverse problems & virtual populations** — calibrate parameter *distributions* to data
:::
::::

[{{< var n_models >}} models, one schema → methods you develop transfer across all of them.]{.lead}

## Validation & limitations — stated plainly

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What these models are**

- mechanistically structured, literature-grounded
- calibrated to **published trial endpoints**
- excellent for hypothesis generation, teaching, MIDD scaffolding
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**What they are *not* (yet)**

- **not** fitted to patient-level data
- **not** for clinical decision-making
- a plausible-looking model can still be wrong → references + human verification are essential; formal validation is future work
:::
::::

> Honesty about scope is part of the method. The LLM drafts; **science validates.**

## Reproducibility & open science

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
- Every model = **code + map + references**, openly in one git repository
- Each is independently **runnable** (`mrgsolve`) and **explorable** (Shiny)
- Full **history**: who / what / when for every parameter — diffable and auditable
- The library **grows in public**, one verifiable commit at a time
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
[100%]{.bignum}<br>
[open · versioned · reproducible]{.muted}
:::
::::

## Traditional vs. LLM-augmented QSP

![Coverage and per-model effort: a step change, not an increment.](assets/plots/traditional_vs_llm.png){width="80%"}

## Future directions

- **Patient-level calibration** — Bayesian fitting to real PK / PD & EHR data
- **Virtual populations** — sample parameter distributions → trial simulation
- **Automated model reduction** — derive minimal mechanistic cores
- **Coupling** to clinical pharmacometric pipelines and electronic health data
- **Formal verification** of generated ODE structure & units
- **Community contributions** — an open, extensible standard for mechanistic disease models

## Conclusion & an open invitation

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
- Drug development needs **mechanism** — expressed as **nonlinear ODE systems**
- QSP supplies it, but has been **slow, scarce, and closed**
- An **autonomous LLM agent** changes the economics: **{{< var n_models >}}** complete, grounded, reproducible models — **and one more nearly every day**
- The result is a **standardized mathematical corpus** of human disease

[This project is **completely open**. If you'd like to try this with us — extend the models, apply your methods (identifiability, model reduction, UQ…), or co-develop new diseases — **we would love to collaborate.**]{.lead}

**Sungpil Han, MD, PhD** · shan@catholic.ac.kr
[github.com/pipetcpt/qsp]{.muted}
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](assets/figs/repo_qr.png){width="48%"}

[Scan for the open repository]{.fineprint}

![](assets/logos/logos_row.png){width="100%"}

**Thank you — questions & collaborators welcome.**
:::
::::
